explanation: The input states that whenever prop_0 holds, this implies that prop_1 is also true ("it yields the condition prop_1"), and also in this circumstance, the condition prop_2 prop_3 should occur (assumed to mean 'prop_2 and prop_3'). Thus, globally, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1, prop_2, and prop_3 must all hold as well.

The statement means that whenever prop_0 is true (which is defined here to mean prop_1 is true), then prop_2 is also true. In LTL, this is expressed as: globally, if prop_0 holds (which is equivalent to prop_1), then prop_2 holds.

The statement says that when prop_0 holds (i.e., some condition is true), then prop_1 (allowing exhalation) should also hold. In LTL, this is best expressed as: globally, whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true at the same moment. This is a safety property and is written using the implication and the globally (G) operator.

The statement describes a condition (prop_0) under which prop_1 and prop_2 should hold. Translating 'When prop_0, prop_1 prop_2' usually means 'globally, whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 and prop_2 should also hold at the same time.' In LTL, this is expressed as G(prop_0 -> (prop_1 & prop_2)).

The statement means: whenever prop_0 holds (which corresponds to the condition V_E<ETS*PeakV_E), prop_1 must also hold at the same time (i.e., prop_1 is true whenever prop_0 is true). In LTL, this is captured as: globally, if prop_0 then prop_1.

The formula describes PSV mode behavior. When prop_0 holds, it yields condition prop_1, and also when that happens, prop_2 must hold. This is a trigger-response where, whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold, and additionally, prop_2 must hold in that same context. So, globally, if prop_0 holds, then both prop_1 and prop_2 must hold.

This statement says that when prop_0 is true, prop_1 becomes true, which in turn enables prop_2. Interpreting 'allowing' as an implication of access or enabling, a safe LTL formulation is: Whenever prop_0 holds, then globally, if prop_1 holds, then prop_2 must hold. This ensures that when the given condition (prop_0) is true, allowing (i.e., if prop_1) prop_2 is enforced as a subsequent guarantee.

The statement says: whenever a given breath's 'prop_0' causes (or yields) that 'prop_1' holds, then 'prop_2' should hold. This is most classically modeled as: whenever (prop_0 & prop_1) happens, then prop_2 must also hold at the same time (since it is for the 'given breath'). So, globally, whenever (prop_0 & prop_1), then prop_2.

This specification states: In PSV Mode, 'when prop_0 (i.e., it yields the condition prop_1), the prop_2.' That is, whenever prop_0 holds (and prop_1, as an explanatory note), then prop_2 should also hold. In LTL, this is expressed as: globally, if prop_0 holds, then prop_2 must also hold.

The phrase says: In 'PSV Mode', when 'prop_0' holds (and that means condition 'prop_1' holds too), then 'prop_2'. This is best captured by: whenever both 'prop_0' and 'prop_1' hold, then 'prop_2' should also hold, i.e., globally, if 'prop_0 & prop_1', then 'prop_2'.
